metadata.summary: Create isolated subagents for autonomous workers with dedicated context windows, model choices, and tool whitelists.

content.title: Creating or Updating Agents
content.summary: Use this reference when creating a new agent or subagent, editing an existing agent, fixing or improving an agent, or adding functionality to an agent. Agents are autonomous workers with isolated contexts, whitelisted tools, and designated roles.

list.item: Parallelizing independent tasks that do not share state, such as three modules implemented simultaneously.
Context isolation: preventing unrelated research from polluting the primary agent's context window.
Role specialization: a reviewer agent that only reads, a coder agent that only writes.
Long-running background work dispatched from an orchestrator, such as research, web scraping, or report generation.
Sequential pipeline stages where each stage needs a clean context: spec, then design, then implement, then test.
Simple single-step tasks that complete in one tool call: agent overhead is not justified.
The subtask requires interactive user input mid-execution: agents run autonomously.
Real-time streaming output is required: Task tool is async; results return on completion.
Task needs to share mutable in-memory state with the parent: agents are isolated, use files as IPC.

section.name: Agents vs Skills vs Commands
section.p: Three main Claude Code components serve different purposes:

rule.statement: Agent: Explicit or auto-delegated invocation. Isolated context. Use for autonomous workers, parallel tasks.
rule.rationale: Agents have dedicated context windows that do not pollute the parent's context. They run asynchronously and enable parallel execution.

rule.statement: Skill: Auto-triggered by context. Shared context. Use for knowledge packages, procedures.
rule.rationale: Skills are invoked automatically based on keywords and integrate into the shared context. They hold reusable knowledge.

rule.statement: Command: Manual /invoke. Shared context. Use for quick actions with arguments.
rule.rationale: Commands are short, stateless actions that the user invokes manually with arguments.

section.name: Agent File Format
section.code:
--- name: agent-name description: Action-oriented description for auto-delegation model: sonnet # sonnet, opus, haiku, inherit tools: [Read, Write, Bash, Grep] # Tool whitelist (least privilege) skills: [skill-name] # Optional skills to load color: "#2F54EB" # UI color version: "1.0.0" disable-model-invocation: false # true = only manual /agent-name permissionMode: default # default, acceptEdits, bypassPermissions ---
# Agent System Prompt
Instructions for the agent...

section.name: Frontmatter Fields
section.p: Required fields:

list.item: name: unique identifier
description: what agent does, critical for auto-delegation

section.p: Model and tools:

list.item: model: sonnet (default), opus, or haiku
tools: whitelist of allowed tools (see Available Tools below)
disallowedTools: blacklist as alternative to whitelist
skills: skills to load in agent context

section.p: Visibility and permissions:

list.item: disable-model-invocation: true means only callable via /agent-name
permissionMode: default (ask for each tool), acceptEdits (auto-accept file edits), bypassPermissions (no prompts, dangerous)

section.p: UI and metadata:

list.item: color: CSS color for identification
version: version string

section.name: Available Tools
section.p: File operations:

list.item: Read: read file contents
Write: create or overwrite files
Edit: targeted string replacements
Glob: find files by pattern
Grep: search with regex

section.p: Execution:

list.item: Bash: shell commands
Task: spawn subagents
TodoWrite: task management

section.p: Web:

list.item: WebFetch: fetch URL content
WebSearch: web search

section.p: Other:

list.item: NotebookEdit: Jupyter notebooks
AskUserQuestion: user input

section.name: Agent Locations

list.item: Personal: ~/.claude/agents/agent-name.md
Project: .claude/agents/agent-name.md

section.p: Project agents override personal agents with the same name.

section.name: Naming Convention
section.p: For shared reusable agents in faion-network, use pattern: faion-{name}-agent, always ending with -agent.

list.item: Role-based: faion-{role}-agent, example faion-pm-agent, faion-ba-agent
Action-based: faion-{action}-agent, example faion-rag-agent, faion-tts-agent
Reviewer: faion-{what}-reviewer-agent, example faion-spec-reviewer-agent
Analyzer: faion-{what}-analyzer-agent, example faion-competitor-analyzer-agent
Builder: faion-{what}-builder-agent, example faion-voice-agent-builder-agent
Generator: faion-{what}-generator-agent, example faion-idea-generator-agent

section.p: For project-specific agents not committed to faion-network, use pattern: {project}-{name}-agent. Add to .gitignore at the same level as .claude/.

section.name: Best Practices
section.p: Design:

list.item: One clear goal per agent
Action-oriented descriptions for auto-delegation
Scope tools per agent (least privilege)

section.p: Tool scoping by role:

list.item: Read-only (reviewers): Read, Grep, Glob with sonnet
Research (analysts): Read, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, WebSearch with sonnet
Code writers: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Glob, Grep with opus
Documentation: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep with sonnet
Orchestrators: Read, Glob, Task, TodoWrite with sonnet

section.p: Context:

list.item: Each agent has isolated context window
Prevents context pollution between tasks
Enables parallel execution

section.name: Prompt Writing Guidelines
section.p: Effective agent prompts include:

list.item: Role definition: You are a {role} with expertise in...
Input/Output contract: What agent receives, what it produces
Step-by-step workflow: Clear phases
Rules and constraints: What to avoid
Error handling: What to do when things fail
Output format: Templates, examples

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: An orchestrator agent with Task tool and read-only tools breaks work into focused subtasks and dispatches them to specialized agents via the Task tool. Each subagent returns structured output in JSON. The orchestrator aggregates results and writes the final artifact. For multi-stage pipelines, each stage's output file is the next stage's input. The file system is the coordination layer.
Recommended subagents:

list.item: faion-pm-agent: project management, issue creation, backlog grooming
faion-ba-agent: requirements analysis, spec drafting
faion-spec-reviewer-agent: validates spec and design documents against quality rubric
faion-task-YOLO-executor-opus-agent: executes implementation tasks with maximum autonomy
faion-idea-generator-agent: generates options and alternatives for architectural decisions
Built-in Explore subagent: fast read-only codebase search

antipattern.description: Each agent instance consumes a full context window.
antipattern.reason: Spawning 10 agents in parallel is costly. Use agent instances sparingly.

antipattern.description: Agent descriptions drive auto-delegation; weak descriptions cause wrong agent selection.
antipattern.reason: Write action-oriented descriptions with clear keywords. Test by asking: would I use this agent if I saw this description?

antipattern.description: bypassPermissions mode in agents runs without guardrails.
antipattern.reason: Dangerous in automated pipelines. Use with extreme caution. Audit before granting.

antipattern.description: Isolated context means agents cannot see the parent's conversation history unless explicitly passed.
antipattern.reason: Pass context via Task tool prompt; reference files rather than inlining large content.

antipattern.description: No built-in retry on agent failure.
antipattern.reason: Orchestrator must handle errors from the Task tool return value.

antipattern.description: Agent files in .claude/agents/ are loaded at Claude Code startup; changes require session restart.
antipattern.reason: Plan session lifecycle carefully when developing agents.

antipattern.description: Circular delegation: agent A spawns agent B which spawns agent A.
antipattern.reason: Causes infinite loops. Design delegation tree carefully.

section.name: Built-in Agents
section.p: Claude Code includes built-in subagents:

list.item: Explore: fast read-only codebase search. Uses Read, Grep, Glob.
Plan: research during plan mode. Uses Read, Grep, Glob, WebSearch.
general-purpose: complex multi-step tasks. Uses all tools.

section.p: Use subagent_type in Task tool to invoke.

list.item: Ask: purpose, tools needed, when to delegate
Ask: local or shared? Local = project-specific, not committed to main repo. Shared = committed to .claude/ repo.
Check if parent project is a git repo; if not, initialize it.
Create file: .claude/agents/agent-name.md
Write frontmatter with minimal tools
Write clear system prompt
If local: add to .gitignore at same level as .claude/
Test with /agent-name

example.title: Research agent
example.code:
--- name: faion-research-agent description: Researches codebase patterns. Use when you need to understand code structure. model: haiku tools: [Read, Glob, Grep, WebFetch] ---
# Research Agent
Research the codebase thoroughly before answering. Use Grep to find patterns, Glob to find files. Report findings in structured format.
example.p: Use Haiku for read-only research tasks to save cost.

example.title: Implementation agent
example.code:
--- name: implementer description: Implements features and fixes bugs. Use when code changes are needed. model: sonnet tools: [Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob] permissionMode: acceptEdits ---
# Implementation Agent
Implement the requested changes following project conventions. Run tests after changes. Commit with descriptive message.
example.p: Reserve Opus for complex architectural decisions. Use Sonnet for implementation.

example.title: Reviewer agent
example.code:
--- name: faion-spec-reviewer-agent description: Validates spec and design documents. Use for quality reviews. model: sonnet tools: [Read, Grep, Glob] ---
# Spec Reviewer Agent
You review specifications and design documents. Check for completeness, clarity, and alignment with standards. Report issues and suggestions in structured format.
example.p: Reviewer agents need only read access. No write or execute permissions.

example.title: Orchestrator agent
example.code:
--- name: faion-orchestrator-agent description: Orchestrates complex workflows. Use for multi-stage pipelines. model: sonnet tools: [Read, Glob, Task, TodoWrite] ---
# Orchestrator Agent
You break complex tasks into focused subtasks. Dispatch each subtask to a specialized subagent using the Task tool. Each subagent returns structured JSON output. Aggregate results and write the final artifact.
example.p: Orchestrators use Task tool to dispatch. They read outputs but do not write directly.

section.name: Agent Patterns and Workflows
section.p: Several reusable patterns emerge for agent-based workflows:
Parallel execution: Main agent spawns multiple independent agents in parallel. Each agent has its own context window. Results are aggregated when all complete.
section.code: Main Agent ├── Agent A (module 1) ─┐ ├── Agent B (module 2) ─┼─ parallel └── Agent C (module 3) ─┘
section.p: Sequential pipeline: Each stage has a focused context and clear input/output contract. Output file from one stage is input to the next stage. The file system is the coordination layer.

Agentic workflow: An orchestrator agent reads configuration or requirements, breaks work into subtasks, dispatches each to a specialized subagent via the Task tool, collects structured results, and writes the final artifact. This pattern scales to complex multi-step workflows.

section.name: Tool Scoping and Least Privilege
section.p: Always whitelist only the tools an agent actually needs. Least privilege prevents accidental side effects:

list.item: Read-only research agent: Read, Grep, Glob, WebFetch with Haiku model
Code reviewer: Read, Grep, Glob with Sonnet model
Code writer: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob with Opus model
Documentation writer: Read, Write, Edit, Glob, Grep with Sonnet model
Orchestrator: Read, Glob, Task, TodoWrite with Sonnet model

section.p: When an agent has unnecessary tools, it becomes unpredictable and may produce unintended side effects. Always ask: does this agent truly need this tool?

section.name: Agent Models and Cost Optimization
section.p: Choose model based on task complexity and cost:

list.item: Haiku: simple tasks, research, read-only, filtering. Fastest and cheapest.
Sonnet: general purpose, implementation, documentation, reviews. Good balance.
Opus: complex reasoning, architecture, design decisions. Most capable but expensive.

section.p: Right-size the model: use Haiku for straightforward work, Sonnet for general purpose, Opus only when reasoning complexity demands it. This directly impacts cost and latency.

section.name: Orchestration with Task Tool
section.p: The Task tool spawns subagents and collects their output. Typical orchestrator pattern:
section.code: Use the Task tool to spawn a research agent: - subagent_type: "general-purpose" - prompt: "Read {files}, extract {information}, write findings to {output_path} as JSON." Then spawn an implementation agent with the findings as input.
section.p: Key rules for orchestration:

list.item: Each subagent receives clear input contract and output path
Subagent writes to a file; orchestrator reads that file
File is the coordination mechanism; the file system is the IPC layer
Pass context via Task tool prompt; reference files rather than inlining large content
Keep Task prompts concise to avoid bloating subagent context
Handle errors from Task tool return value; no built-in retry mechanism

section.name: CLI Tools and Integration
section.p: Agents work with external CLI tools:

list.item: claude CLI: Launch Claude Code, list agents, invoke /agent-name
gh: GitHub CLI used inside agents for PR and issue operations
jq: Parse JSON output from agents in shell scripts
flock: Serialize concurrent agent writes to shared files

section.p: Services that work well with agents:

list.item: Claude Code: agents are first-class primitives
Anthropic Agent SDK: alternative for programmatic agent orchestration
GitHub Actions: agents can trigger workflows, push commits, create PRs
Linear: agents create and update issues via Linear API

antipattern.description: Agent spawning 10 parallel instances blows context budget.
antipattern.reason: Each agent has a full context window. Use parallelism judiciously. Prefer sequential pipelines when possible.

antipattern.description: Agent description is vague; auto-delegation selects wrong agent.
antipattern.reason: Write action-oriented descriptions with clear keywords. Test by asking: would I manually invoke this agent if I saw this description?
Agents run without guardrails. Dangerous in automated pipelines. Use default or acceptEdits instead.

antipattern.description: Inlining large content in Task prompt instead of referencing a file.
antipattern.reason: This bloats subagent context. Instead, write content to a file; subagent reads via Read tool.

antipattern.description: Agent file loaded at startup; changes require session restart.
antipattern.reason: Plan session lifecycle when developing agents. Consider restarting for safety.

antipattern.description: Circular delegation: agent A spawns B which spawns A.
antipattern.reason: Results in infinite loops. Design delegation tree as a DAG with clear endpoints.

antipattern.description: Agent with Bash tool and bypassPermissions mode.
antipattern.reason: Can delete files, push to remote, send network requests. Audit before granting.

antipattern.description: Loading too many skills into agent's context frontmatter.
antipattern.reason: Inflates context and degrades instruction-following. Load only essential skills.

list.item: One clear goal per agent. Agents that do everything succeed at nothing.
Whitelist only tools the agent needs. Least privilege prevents side effects.
Use model: haiku for read-only research agents. Reserve opus for architecture and spec.
Put shared context in agent's system prompt, not the Task prompt.
Write agent output to files, not stdout. Orchestrator reads files.
Prefer permissionMode: acceptEdits over bypassPermissions for file-writing agents.
Name agents with action verbs in description: Analyzes X, Implements Y, Reviews Z.
Design orchestration as a DAG: clear inputs, outputs, and order.
Test descriptions: would I use this agent if I saw this description?
